title: Rosehip facial soap bar
subtitle: A small-batch cold-process facial bar with rosehip, sweet almond, and coconut oils at 5% superfat
excerpt: A 275 g oil batch designed for facial use, yielding four smaller bars. Rosehip oil contributes linoleic acid and is included at 18% of the oil blend; sweet almond oil adds gentle emolliency. This is a high-superfat, high-linoleic recipe -- it produces a softer bar than a body soap and is best cured for the full six weeks.
sourceNotes: SAP values used in calculations: sweet almond oil 0.136, coconut oil 0.190, rosehip oil 0.135, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied. Reference: The Handcrafter's Companion, Anne L. Watson. Rosehip oil in cold-process soap: linoleic-acid contribution and shortened shelf life noted in soap-craft literature.

recipe.yieldDescription: 4 facial soap bars (275 g oil batch)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Always re-run any revised oil weights through a lye calculator before scaling -- do not simply multiply the lye weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-process soap requires a 48-hour rest in the mould, then a minimum 4-week cure. A 6-week cure is recommended for this recipe because of the high linoleic-acid content.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep for one year on an open soap dish. High-linoleic recipes oxidise faster than olive-heavy bars -- discard if bars develop a rancid smell or orange oily spots (DOS).

glossaryTerms.definition: The alkali that reacts with oils in cold-process soap making (saponification). Always add lye to water, never water to lye. Wear goggles, gloves, and ensure ventilation during the lye-water mixing step.

content.text: This recipe is sized as a small 275 g oil batch to yield four facial bars rather than a full loaf of body bars. Facial soap benefits from a higher-linoleic, lower-coconut oil profile than body soap -- coconut oil is a strong cleanser that can strip delicate facial skin if used at high percentages. Rosehip oil is the conditioning feature here; it will also shorten the bar's shelf life somewhat, as it is prone to oxidation. Handle

Lye causes chemical burns on contact with skin and eyes. Wear safety goggles and nitrile gloves throughout. Work in a ventilated area -- the lye-water mixture releases heat and fumes when first mixed. Add lye to water, never the reverse. Keep children and pets out of the workspace during the lye steps.

items.prepNote: 99% purity cosmetic-grade NaOH; calculated at 5% superfat
about 2.9% of oil weight; add at light trace

content.text: Wearing goggles and gloves, weigh 96 g of distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Add the lye slowly to the water, stirring as you go. The mixture will heat sharply; do not breathe over the pitcher. Set aside to cool to 38 to 45°C. A small batch like this cools faster than a full 500 g batch.

Melt the coconut oil. Combine with the sweet almond oil, rosehip oil, and castor oil. Allow to cool to 38 to 45°C. Add the rosehip oil last to minimise heat exposure -- rosehip is sensitive to temperature and oxidises faster than other oils in this blend.

Pour the lye solution slowly into the oil phase. Use the stick blender carefully in very short bursts -- small batches reach trace quickly and you can easily over-blend. Aim for light trace. Add the rose geranium essential oil if using and stir through by hand.

Pour into the silicone loaf mould -- a small batch will only fill a portion of the mould. Cover and insulate with a towel for 48 hours. Unmould and slice into four bars with the wire cutter. Place cut-side up on a rack to cure.

Cure for six weeks for this recipe. The high proportion of unsaturated oils (rosehip, sweet almond) means the bar needs the full cure time to firm up and for the lye to neutralise completely. Test with pH paper -- finished soap reads 9 to 10. Store finished bars on an open rack, not in a closed container, to reduce the risk of rancidity.
